title: When Should You Use AI? New Study Says Timing Is Everything for Critical Thinking
summary: Next time you're tempted to immediately ask ChatGPT for help with a tough problem, consider slowing down. A new study suggests that the timing of AI use can significantly impact your critical thinking skills. Researchers led by computer scientist Mina Lee at the University of Chicago randomly assigned 393 participants to one of eight groups, varying the time they had (30 minutes or 10 minutes) and when they could access OpenAI's GPT-4o chatbot: early, continuously, late, or not at all. Participants played the role of a city council member deciding whether to accept a company's proposal to address water contamination, using a set of seven documents to write an essay explaining their decision. The results, presented April 14 at the 2026 CHI conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems in Barcelona, revealed a clear pattern. Among those with sufficient time (30 minutes), the group that had late access to the chatbot—meaning they worked on the problem independently first—scored highest on essay quality, incorporating more valid arguments and textual references. They also showed less "myside bias," meaning they considered more perspectives. Interestingly, the group with no chatbot access at all performed best on recalling information from the documents. This aligns with research on two types of learning: slow, effortful reasoning versus fast, automatic thinking. "Participants who had time to reason through the material on their own before using AI did best because they had already engaged in that slower, more deliberate learning," explained Barbara Oakley, a systems engineer and education expert at Oakland University. However, the picture changed under time pressure. In the groups given only 10 minutes, those who used the chatbot early scored highest on their essays. Lee cautioned that this comes with a trade-off: "When you are under time pressure and use AI to boost your performance, then you are basically risking just taking and using the AI's framing, and that reduces the kinds of arguments that you make and your engagement with the documents." The study underscores the need for AI literacy. As Lee puts it, people must be aware of what they're signing up for when they turn to chatbots at different stages of problem-solving. The findings are a first step toward understanding how time constraints shape our reliance on AI—and how we can use these tools without sacrificing our own thinking skills.
why_it_matters: The study reveals that blindly relying on AI can weaken our own reasoning muscles, much like never exercising your body—as AI tools become normal in school and work, learning to balance their use is a critical skill for staying sharp and independent.

keywords.explanation: The tendency to favor arguments that support one's own view rather than considering all perspectives.
Slow, effortful thinking where you carefully work through a problem yourself.
Knowing how to use AI tools wisely, understanding both their strengths and risks.
Having a very limited amount of time to finish a task, which can force quick decisions.
A professional who designs and analyzes complex systems, often combining technology and human behavior.
A compromise where you gain something but lose something else in return.

questions.question: Under sufficient time (30 minutes), which group scored highest on essay quality?
options: The group with late access to the chatbot
The group with early access to the chatbot
The group with continuous access to the chatbot
The group with no access to the chatbot
questions.correct_answer: The group with late access to the chatbot

questions.question: What bias did the late-access group show less of?
options: Myside bias
Confirmation bias
Anchoring bias
Hindsight bias

questions.question: Which group performed best on recalling information from the documents?
options: The group with no chatbot access
The group with early access
The group with continuous access
The group with late access
questions.correct_answer: The group with no chatbot access

questions.question: According to Barbara Oakley, what type of learning led to better essay results?
options: Slow, deliberate learning
Fast, automatic thinking
Collaborative brainstorming
Memorization without reflection

questions.question: Under time pressure (10 minutes), which group scored highest on their essays?
options: The group that used the chatbot early
The group that used the chatbot late
The group that never used the chatbot
The group that used the chatbot continuously
questions.correct_answer: The group that used the chatbot early

questions.question: What trade-off did Mina Lee mention about using AI early under time pressure?
options: You risk adopting the AI's framing and reducing your own arguments
You save time but lose the ability to spell correctly
You get more accurate information but slower writing speed
You produce longer essays but with fewer facts
questions.correct_answer: You risk adopting the AI's framing and reducing your own arguments

background_read: The study taps into a well-known psychological theory called dual-process thinking, popularized by Nobel laureate Daniel Kahneman, which says humans have a fast, intuitive 'System 1' and a slow, analytical 'System 2.'

Mina Lee (a computer scientist at the University of Chicago who specializes in human-AI interaction) presented the findings at the 2026 CHI conference, the premier international gathering for research on how people interact with technology, held this year in Barcelona.

Previous experiments have shown that when people use GPS devices too often, their spatial memory weakens; similarly, 'cognitive offloading' to AI can weaken recall and reasoning, a pattern confirmed by the fact that the no-chatbot group had the best recall.

Barbara Oakley (a systems engineer and education expert at Oakland University and author of 'A Mind for Numbers') noted that the groups who reasoned independently first had time to build mental models, which made their later AI use more effective.
Article_Structure: HOOK: "Next time you're tempted to immediately ask ChatGPT for help with a tough problem, consider slowing down."
├─ RESEARCHER: Mina Lee and team at University of Chicago
├─ PARTICIPANTS: 393 people, randomly assigned to 8 groups
├─ VARIABLES: Time (30 min vs. 10 min) and AI access timing (early, continuous, late, none)
└─ TASK: Play a city council member deciding on a water contamination proposal using 7 documents, then write an essay
├─ WITH ENOUGH TIME: Late AI access produced best essays—more valid arguments, more references, less myside bias
└─ UNDER TIME PRESSURE: Early AI access scored highest on essay quality
SURPRISE: The no-chatbot group had the best recall of document information
THE REASON: Slow, deliberate learning before using AI builds understanding; early AI use leads to borrowing the AI's framing
EXPERT QUOTE: Barbara Oakley explains how independent reasoning first strengthens learning
TRADE-OFF IDENTIFIED: Using AI early under time pressure boosts performance but risks losing your own arguments
CONCLUSION: As AI spreads, people need 'AI literacy'—knowing when to lean on it and when to think for themselves

perspectives.perspective: Positive: Smart timing can supercharge learning
perspectives.description: The results show that AI isn't the enemy of critical thinking—it's all about when you use it. Strategically delaying AI could help students build deeper understanding before turning to it for polish.

perspectives.perspective: Negative: A trap for busy people
perspectives.description: Under time pressure, early AI use boosted performance, but at the cost of adopting the machine's viewpoint. In high-stakes settings like journalism or medicine, this reliance could spread errors and narrow perspectives.

The study elegantly demonstrates a classic cognitive pattern: when people engage in effortful reasoning first, they are less likely to fall for biased shortcuts. This aligns with decades of research showing that deep processing builds better retention.

Future AI interfaces could be designed to encourage 'thinking time' before offering suggestions. Schools might begin teaching 'AI timing' as part of digital literacy, much like we teach note-taking strategies.
